CARVEYANCEIcons · The Love Letters
The car that almost broke BMW

The Beautiful Mistake

It was built to save BMW. Instead it nearly sank the company that made it — and quietly became the most romantic car Munich has ever built.

By CarveyanceBMW 507 · 1956–19592026

The BMW 507 is one of those cars that looks as if it should have saved the company.

That is the first trick.

You see it sitting there — low, clean, balanced, almost impossibly graceful — and you think: of course this worked. Of course America bought them by the thousands. Of course BMW became the romantic German sports-car house on the strength of it. Of course every 1950s movie star, racing driver, industrialist and handsome troublemaker wanted one.

And then the truth arrives, wearing a beautifully tailored suit and carrying a bankruptcy notice.

The BMW 507 did not save BMW. It nearly helped sink it. And somehow, that makes it even more romantic.

Because the 507 is not merely a car. It is an automotive love affair that went financially wrong. The beautiful mistake. The expensive dinner. The handwritten letter sent to the wrong address. The car that failed in period, survived through myth, and later became one of the most valuable, beautiful and emotionally important BMWs ever made.

It is BMW's moonlit roadster. It is also proof that not every failure dies. Some failures age into art.

Why BMW Made It

The 507 came from a very specific postwar moment.

BMW in the early 1950s was not the BMW we know now. It was not yet the company of the 2002, the 3 Series, the M5, the M1, or the phrase “Ultimate Driving Machine.” It was recovering, uncertain, trying to re-establish itself after the war. Its first postwar car, the 501, was a large luxury saloon — dignified, well made, and not remotely a commercial breakthrough. By BMW's own account, the company's position grew more precarious as the decade wore on.

Then came Max Hoffman, the Austrian-born, New York-based importer who seemed to know what Americans wanted before Americans did. Hoffman had the ear of Mercedes-Benz, Porsche, Alfa Romeo and BMW, and a hand in the creation of the Mercedes 300SL roadster, the Porsche 356 Speedster and, now, this. His idea for BMW was simple and brilliant: build a glamorous open sports car for wealthy Americans. Not as expensive as the 300SL. Not as ordinary as an MG or a Triumph. Something in between — exotic, German, V8-powered, elegant and desirable. He believed he could sell five thousand a year at around $5,000 apiece, and personally committed to a large order.

BMW showed a prototype at New York's Waldorf-Astoria in the summer of 1955, and again that autumn in Frankfurt. The intention was never modest. The 507 was meant to be a statement — a halo car, a message to America that said: BMW is back, and BMW can be beautiful.

The Design: Goertz and the Dream of the Isar

The shape belongs to Albrecht Graf von Goertz, a freelance industrial designer who had trained under Raymond Loewy and who, tellingly, had never designed a complete car. He drew fountain pens and furniture; later he would draw the Datsun 240Z. In between, at Hoffman's insistence, he was handed the 503 and the 507 — and he made, in BMW's own words, a “picture-perfect sports car.”

That is not marketing exaggeration.

The 507 is beautiful in a way that seems almost effortless. It does not have the angry tension of a modern performance car. It needs no wings, vents, slashes, carbon or a grille large enough to process livestock. It is low, long and calm. The kidney grille is delicate. The side vents are jewellery. The rear haunches are soft but athletic. The windscreen is graceful. The proportions are very nearly perfect. It looks less designed than composed.

Munich noticed. The press of the day reached for phrases like the “BMW sensation” and the “Dream of the Isar,” the Isar being the river that runs through the city. That second name is perfect. The 507 really does feel like Munich dreaming of California.

The Engineering: Not Radical, But Very Special

Here is where the 507 becomes interesting.

It was not a technological moonshot like the Mercedes-Benz 300SL Gullwing. No fuel injection, no spaceframe, no theatrical doors, no racing-car strangeness. The 507 was more traditional than that — but it was beautifully assembled from serious ingredients. BMW built it on a shortened version of the 502/503 chassis, roughly 35.5 cm tighter in the wheelbase, and clothed it in a body of hand-formed aluminium. Because those bodies were beaten by hand, no two 507s were exactly identical — right down to the soft-tops, each of which had to be made to fit its own individual car. A roof from one would not sit properly on the next.

That is the kind of detail that sounds romantic now and was financially catastrophic then.

Underneath sat BMW's light-alloy 3.2-litre V8 — 3,168 cc, overhead valves, a pair of Zenith carburettors — lifted from the 502 saloon and breathed on to make around 150 hp at 5,000 rpm, a little more than the sedan. Drive went rearward through a close-ratio four-speed ZF manual, with independent torsion-bar front suspension and a live rear axle. It was not featherweight despite the aluminium: it tipped the scales at around 2,900 lb (1,330 kg). This was not a little British roadster. It was a substantial, expensive German GT with an open body and an American-friendly V8.

It looks less designed than composed.

Specs at a Glance

BMW 507 — the essentials
BMW 507Key details
Production1956–1959
Engine3,168 cc aluminium OHV V8
Power~150 hp at 5,000 rpm
Torque~173 lb-ft
Transmission4-speed ZF manual
LayoutFront engine, rear-wheel drive
BodyHand-formed aluminium
Weight~2,900 lb (1,330 kg)
0–60 mph~11 sec in period testing
Top speed~122–125 mph (BMW quoted ~200 km/h)
Price when newDM 26,500 · ~$9,000 in the US, rising to ~$10,500
Built252 cars (254 with the two prototypes) · ~202 survive

The production figure is one of those charming classic-car arguments. BMW's model-series records land on 251 by the end of the run; the wider collector world generally says 252, or 254 once you count the two prototypes. The difference is bookkeeping — how you tally the pre-production cars. Either way, the honest answer is: not many. Around 202 are known to survive.

That scarcity is a big reason so few people know the 507 well. It was not common when new, and it was rarely seen afterward. It lived in collections, stories, auction catalogues and photographs of famous people looking better than the rest of us.

How It Drove

The BMW 507 was not a razor. It was a romantic grand touring roadster. That distinction matters.

A 300SL felt like racing technology dressed for the road. A Porsche 356 felt lighter, smaller, sharper, more minimalist. A Corvette felt more American — louder, cheaper, brasher, more muscular. The 507 was none of those. It was about elegance, sound, warmth and flow. Drive one today and it does not snap at the throttle; it gathers. You sit low behind that delicate windscreen, look down the long hood, hear the V8 clear its throat, and the car begins to move like a gentleman removing his jacket before doing something unexpectedly athletic.

Top Gear drove one decades later and wrote that once past 2,000 rpm the engine “just sings,” gathering speed usefully rather than instantly, with real weight in the steering and genuine rigidity in the body. That sounds exactly right. The numbers were never 300SL numbers — around 11 seconds to 60 mph in period tests, a touch over 120 mph flat out, with a taller final drive available for those who wanted more autobahn and less acceleration. But speed was never quite the point.

Think less knife between the teeth. More silk scarf at 120 mph.

What Was Incredible

The first thing is obvious: beauty. The 507 may be the most beautiful BMW ever made. That is subjective, but not by much. It has a visual confidence BMW has rarely attempted since — the Z8 later paid it open homage — and it earns that confidence from every angle.

The second is rarity. With roughly 252 built and around 202 surviving, the 507 is rare enough to feel mythical but not so obscure that nobody cares. It sits in the perfect collector zone: famous, beautiful, important and almost impossible to encounter by accident.

The third is the V8. In 1950s Europe, where punitive engine taxes kept most sports cars small, a light-alloy BMW V8 in an open two-seater was genuinely special. It never made the 507 brutally fast, but it gave the car a character the smaller British and German roadsters could not touch.

The fourth is image. Even in failure, the 507 gave BMW something priceless: glamour. It proved BMW could build not just a clever sedan or a sturdy motorcycle or a tiny economy car, but an object of desire. BMW's later identity would rest on engineering and driver involvement — but the 507 added something softer and more dangerous. Romance.

What Was Not So Great

The first problem was price. Hoffman imagined a car around $5,000. Reality pushed it far higher — nearer $8,988 at launch, climbing to roughly $10,500 by 1959. In today's money that is somewhere around $105,000–$125,000, depending on how you count. Worse, it dropped the 507 within touching distance of the Mercedes-Benz 300SL, a car with a far stronger racing pedigree and more advanced engineering.

The second problem was the cost to build. Hand-formed aluminium bodies are wonderful if you are writing poetry. They are less wonderful if you are running a small car company with a cash-flow problem. The 507 was simply too labour-intensive and too expensive to scale.

The third was performance relative to price. The 507 was quick, but not quick enough to embarrass the 300SL, and not cheap enough to be the affordable exotic. It sat in the middle — exactly where Hoffman wanted it — right up until its price climbed out of the middle and into the danger zone.

And the fourth was timing. Wealthy buyers could have a 300SL. Less wealthy ones could have a Corvette, a Porsche or a British roadster for a fraction of the money. The 507 was too expensive to be the bargain and not outrageous enough to defeat the Mercedes. It was stunning. It was desirable. It was just not the right business answer. That is the tragedy.

Did It Hurt BMW, or Elevate It?

Yes.

That is the answer.

It hurt BMW financially in the short term. The company's broader 1950s position was already fragile, and the 507 did not fix it — in fact the losses helped push BMW to the edge. Things became precarious enough that, in late 1959, Daimler-Benz tabled a restructuring offer. BMW survived as an independent company only because shareholders and workers resisted the plan and Herbert Quandt chose to expand his investment rather than walk away.

But the 507 elevated BMW spiritually. That sounds dramatic. It is also true.

The modern BMW business was not built by the 507. It was built by the 700 and, above all, the Neue Klasse — the 1500, launched at the 1961 Frankfurt show, which re-established BMW as a serious modern carmaker with orders outstripping the factory. The Neue Klasse made BMW viable. The 507 made BMW beautiful. The 2002 and 3 Series later made it sharp and cool; the M cars made it fierce. But the 507 is the proof that BMW once built a car simply because it wanted to be seen as glamorous and alive. It failed as commerce. It succeeded as mythology.

Famous Owners: How the 507 Became a Celebrity

A car this beautiful and this impractical attracted exactly the ownership list you would expect.

The most famous owner was Elvis Presley. Stationed in Germany with the US Army, he took on a 1957 car, chassis 70079, finished in Feather White — a car that had already been raced by Hans Stuck and used as a BMW press demonstrator. The best endorsement in the story is not about torsional rigidity. It is that Elvis, by all accounts, drove it like hell on the autobahn and promptly wanted one of his own.

There is a lovely wrinkle, too. His European fans kept leaving lipstick messages on the white paint while the car sat outside his house in Bad Nauheim — so Elvis simply had it painted red. Decades and several owners later, BMW itself tracked the car down, restored it, and returned it to its original Feather White. Munich, quietly, reclaiming its own ghost.

The rest of the register is just as glamorous: Fred Astaire, Alain Delon, Ursula Andress, Prince Rainier of Monaco, King Constantine II of Greece, the Aga Khan, and a young Bernie Ecclestone among them. But the most poignant 507 of all belonged to John Surtees — handed to him by Count Agusta as a reward for winning the 1956 500cc World Motorcycle Championship. Surtees worked with Dunlop to develop disc brakes for it and kept the car until he died. When it finally sold at auction in 2018, it made roughly $5 million — the most ever paid for a 507, and the most ever paid for any BMW. One of the most beautiful BMWs ever built, owned by the only man to win world championships on both two wheels and four. Cars do not get much more romantic than that.

Not “Elvis appreciated the torsional rigidity.” Elvis drove it like hell — and ordered one.

How It Stacked Up

507 vs. Mercedes-Benz 300SL

This was the unavoidable comparison. The 300SL was more advanced, more exotic, more directly connected to motorsport, and faster. It had fuel injection, racing roots and, in Gullwing form, doors that made every arrival feel staged by NASA and Cartier. The 507 was simpler, softer, more relaxed — the more practical of the two, with more luggage space and easier access, if less outright pace. The Mercedes made you feel like you were driving a racing machine let loose on the public road. The BMW made you feel like you were touring Europe on a long weekend with no return date.

507 vs. Porsche 356

The Porsche was lighter, simpler, cheaper and more intimate — delicate and efficient in a way the BMW never tried to be. The 507 was larger, pricier, more glamorous, more of a grand tourer, with a V8 the Porsche could not answer. But it lacked the 356's light-footed purity. The Porsche was a scalpel. The BMW was a silver cigarette case with an engine.

507 vs. Chevrolet Corvette

Here the business case wobbled. A Corvette offered V8 power and open-top glamour for far less money. It had none of the BMW's delicacy or European elegance, but it had American confidence, improving performance and a price that made sense. A Corvette was rock and roll in a diner parking lot. The 507 was jazz at midnight in a hotel bar. Elvis, wonderfully, understood both.

507 vs. the British roadsters

Against MGs, Triumphs and Austin-Healeys, the 507 was from another social class entirely — vastly more expensive, more sophisticated, more luxurious. But those British cars had accessibility on their side. They were attainable dreams. The BMW was a dream behind glass. Which may be exactly why the British sports cars became common memories, and the 507 became legend.

Was It Actually Great?

This is the important question.

As a business product? No. As a pure performance machine? Not quite. Measured against the 300SL on engineering alone, it was not the technological king.

But as an object? As a statement? As a piece of BMW's soul? Absolutely.

The 507 is great precisely because it is not merely rational. It represents a version of BMW that was willing to overreach — and that is rare. Most companies fail by making dull things nobody wants. BMW nearly failed here by making something too beautiful, too expensive, too difficult and too ambitious for its own reality. That is a very different kind of failure. Arguably a nobler one.

What Does One Cost Now?

In 2026, the 507 is a fully-fledged seven-figure collector car. Hagerty's public guide puts a good example around $1.4 million, with adders for a factory hardtop, original Rudge knock-offs and factory disc brakes. Classic.com's market page shows an average of roughly $1.94 million, a record of $2.315 million (a 1957 Series II with hardtop), and plenty of recent activity: a 1959 car sold for $1,512,500 at Mecum's Kissimmee sale in January 2026, and a 1957 example brought about €1.47 million in Munich in late 2025. Dealer asking prices now stretch toward $2.9 million.

And the ceiling is higher still. The Surtees Touring car — that world-champion provenance — remains the high-water mark at around $5 million.

BMW 507 · 2026 market
Condition / provenanceApproximate 2026 value
Driver / good condition$1.4M – $1.6M
Excellent, correct, desirable spec$1.7M – $2.1M
Exceptional / concours / provenance$2.3M+
Record (Surtees Touring car, 2018)~$5M

The irony is almost painful. The car Hoffman wanted to sell for $5,000 now trades for around two million dollars. The car that was too expensive to succeed became too rare to ignore.

Why We Don't Know It Better

Maybe people do not know the 507 because it was not that great — or maybe because so few were made. The answer is both, but not evenly.

It was never “not great.” It was great in a narrow, emotional, aesthetic way. But it was not successful, and because so few were built, it never entered daily automotive consciousness. The 300SL became myth through racing and spectacle. The 356 became myth through volume and evolution. The Corvette became myth through American culture. The 507 became myth quietly — almost privately. It was a car for the few. Then the few became famous. Then the car became priceless.

The Love Letter

The BMW 507 is not the kind of car you admire because it won the spreadsheet. It did not. It is the kind of car you admire because it reminds you that car companies are at their best when they occasionally lose their minds.

There is something deeply human in it. BMW needed a hit and made a sculpture. It needed volume and made 250-odd hand-built dreams. It needed survival and instead wrote a love letter to America in aluminium, V8 noise and Goertz lines. Was that foolish? Yes. Was it glorious? Also yes.

The 507 belongs less to the showroom than to memory. It is Elvis on the autobahn. It is John Surtees with a roadster worthy of a world champion. It is Munich trying to seduce California — a German car with Italian grace, American ambition and a tragic accountant standing somewhere in the background, holding his head.

It did not make BMW rich. It did not save BMW. It did something stranger. It gave BMW a ghost. And that ghost still haunts every beautiful BMW roadster that has come since. The Z8 knew it. The Z3 wished it did. The Z4 occasionally remembers. Every time BMW reaches for grace instead of mere aggression, the 507 is there in the room, leaning against the wall, looking impossibly expensive and slightly amused.

The BMW 507 was a failure. The BMW 507 was a masterpiece. Both things are true. That is why we still talk about it.
